title: Token-Aware Context Building
text: LLMs finite context windows. typical model accepts 4,000 128,000 tokens, every token spend context token spend model’s response. building RAG applications, often retrieved content fits window – system prompts, retrieved chunks, conversation history, user instructions compete space. Naive approaches (concatenate everything, hope fits) fail predictable ways: either prompt truncated silently, model ignores information buried middle long context. securecontext’s context_builder() solves priority-based token budget gives explicit control gets included clear reporting gets dropped.

title: Token-Aware Context Building
text: builder follows simple algorithm: Items highest priority numbers included first. remaining budget accommodate next item, item (lower-priority items) excluded. builder reports lists, always know exactly LLM see .
code: +------------------+                     | Set token budget |                     | (max_tokens)     |                     +--------+---------+                              |                     +--------v---------+                     | Sort items by    |                     | priority (desc)  |                     +--------+---------+                              |                 +------------v------------+                 | For each item (highest  |                 | priority first):        |                 |                         |                 |  Estimate token count   |                 |         |               |                 |    Fits in budget?       |                 |    /           \         |                 |  YES           NO       |                 |   |             |        |                 | Include      Exclude    |                 | (deduct      (record    |                 |  tokens)      label)    |                 +------------+------------+                              |                     +--------v---------+                     | Return:          |                     | - context string |                     | - included list  |                     | - excluded list  |                     | - total_tokens   |                     +------------------+

title: Token-Aware Context Building
text: Create builder token budget, add content priorities, build final context string. 100-token budget, system prompt (priority 10) always included. Retrieved chunks packed priority order budget exhausted. $excluded field tells exactly chunks dropped, valuable debugging logging production agents.
code:
library(securecontext)  cb <- context_builder(max_tokens = 100)  # Add content with different priorities (higher = included first) cb <- cb_add(cb, "You are a helpful assistant.", priority = 10, label = "system") cb <- cb_add(cb,   "R is great for statistics and data visualization.",   priority = 5, label = "retrieved_chunk_1" ) cb <- cb_add(cb,   "Python is popular for machine learning and web development.",   priority = 4, label = "retrieved_chunk_2" ) cb <- cb_add(cb,   "Julia offers high performance for numerical computing workloads.",   priority = 3, label = "retrieved_chunk_3" )  result <- cb_build(cb)  cat("Assembled context:
") cat(result$context, "

") cat("Included:", paste(result$included, collapse = ", "), "
") cat("Excluded:", paste(result$excluded, collapse = ", "), "
") cat("Total tokens:", result$total_tokens, "
")

title: Token-Aware Context Building
text: Choosing priority values design decision depends application. common patterns: Fixed tiers assign priorities content type: Dynamic priorities use retrieval scores directly. retrieve chunks cosine similarity scores, can pass scores priorities relevant chunks included first:
code:
cb <- context_builder(max_tokens = 500)  # System prompt gets highest priority -- always included cb <- cb_add(cb, "You are an R expert.", priority = 10, label = "system")  # Retrieved chunks get decreasing priority by relevance score hits <- retrieve(ret, "statistical models", k = 5) for (i in seq_len(nrow(hits))) {   chunk_text <- hits$id[i]  # or look up the original text   cb <- cb_add(cb, chunk_text, priority = hits$score[i], label = hits$id[i]) }  result <- cb_build(cb) cat("Included:", paste(result$included, collapse = ", "), "
") cat("Excluded:", paste(result$excluded, collapse = ", "), "
") cat("Total tokens:", result$total_tokens, "
")

title: Token-Aware Context Building
text: multi-turn conversation, typically rebuild context turn – retrieved chunks change, conversation history grows, system prompt may updated. Use cb_reset() clear items reuse builder without re-specifying token budget. avoids overhead creating new builder object turn makes intent clear: fresh context assembly new turn.
code: cb2 <- cb_reset(cb) cb2 <- cb_add(cb2, "New system prompt.", priority = 10, label = "system_v2") result2 <- cb_build(cb2) cat("After reset -- included:", paste(result2$included, collapse = ", "), "\n")

title: Token-Aware Context Building
text: common case “retrieve chunks build context string,” context_for_chat() combines steps single call. retrieves top-k chunks retriever packs token-limited string. hood, creates context builder, adds retrieved chunk similarity score priority, returns built result. convenience wrapper; need control (e.g., adding system prompt conversation history alongside retrieved chunks), use builder directly.
code: result <- context_for_chat(ret, "statistics", max_tokens = 2000) cat(result$context)

title: RAG-Enabled Agents
text: LLM agent retrieves information knowledge base answering can ground responses documents rather relying training data alone. retrieval alone enough – also need decide retrieve, much context include, store agent learns. orchestration concerns. securecontext handles document ingestion, embedding, vector search, token-aware context assembly. orchestr defines agent workflows directed graphs, manages state nodes, routes execution. Together, let build RAG agents retrieval explicit graph node inputs, outputs, tests. sections connect two packages using memory adapter pattern graph-based retrieve--generate workflows. securecontext basics, see vignette("securecontext") vignette("retrieval-workflows"). orchestr basics, see vignette("quickstart", package = "orchestr").

title: RAG-Enabled Agents
text: Start creating documents, chunking , loading retriever. pipeline vignette("retrieval-workflows"), repeated completeness. Everything runs locally; external API calls required.
code: library(securecontext) library(orchestr) library(ellmer)  # Create documents from your corpus docs <- list(   document("R provides extensive facilities for statistical computing and graphics. Linear models, time-series analysis, classification, and clustering are all available out of the box.",     metadata = list(source = "r-intro", topic = "statistics")),   document("The tidyverse is a collection of R packages for data science. Core packages include ggplot2, dplyr, tidyr, readr, and purrr.",     metadata = list(source = "r-intro", topic = "tidyverse")),   document("Shiny is an R package for building interactive web applications. It combines R's analytical power with modern web UI components.",     metadata = list(source = "r-intro", topic = "shiny")) )  # Build embedder from the corpus corpus <- vapply(docs, function(d) d@text, character(1)) embedder <- embed_tfidf(corpus)  # Create vector store and retriever vs <- vector_store$new(dims = embedder@dims) ret <- retriever(vs, embedder) add_documents(ret, docs, chunk_strategy = "sentence")

title: RAG-Enabled Agents
text: orchestr agents expect memory backend get() set() methods. securecontext’s knowledge_store provides persistent key-value storage backed JSONL, interface match orchestr expects box. as_orchestr_memory() function bridges gap. adapter pattern simple: wrap securecontext object thin layer exposes interface another package expects. underlying storage, persistence, search capabilities knowledge store fully preserved; adapter just translates method calls. underlying JSONL file persists across R sessions, agent memory survives restarts. can also access knowledge store directly (via ks) use features like $search() $list() part orchestr memory interface.
code: # Create a persistent knowledge store ks <- knowledge_store$new(path = "agent-memory.jsonl")  # Wrap it for orchestr mem <- as_orchestr_memory(ks)  # The adapter exposes get/set -- the same interface orchestr expects mem$set("user.name", "Alice") mem$set("session.topic", "data analysis") mem$get("user.name") #> [1] "Alice"

title: RAG-Enabled Agents
text: core RAG pattern : retrieve relevant context LLM call. orchestr’s graph_builder(), wire two-node graph: retrieval node followed agent node. following diagram shows flow: retrieval node runs securecontext pipeline (retrieve + context build). agent node takes context passes LLM alongside user’s question. separating concerns distinct graph nodes, step independently testable replaceable. Every user query first passes retrieval node, searches vector store assembles context string. agent node answers using context. context builder enforces token budget, agent node always receives prompt fits within model’s context window.
code:
+----------+      +---------+      +-----+   | retrieve | ---> |  agent  | ---> | END |   +----------+      +---------+      +-----+        |                  |   Searches vector    Uses retrieved   store, builds      context to   token-limited      answer query   context string     via LLM # Node 1: retrieve relevant chunks and build context retrieve_node <- function(state, config) {   query <- state$messages[[length(state$messages)]]    # Retrieve and assemble token-limited context   result <- context_for_chat(ret, query, max_tokens = 2000, k = 5)    list(context = result$context) }  # Node 2: LLM agent that uses the retrieved context agent_node <- function(state, config) {   context <- state$context %||% ""   query <- state$messages[[length(state$messages)]]    prompt <- paste0(     "Use the following context to answer the question.

",     "Context:
", context, "

",     "Question: ", query   )    chat <- chat_anthropic(system_prompt = "You are a helpful R assistant.")   response <- chat$chat(prompt)    list(messages = list(response)) }  # Wire the graph: retrieve -> agent -> END schema <- state_schema(   messages = "append:list",   context = "character" )  graph <- graph_builder(state_schema = schema) graph$add_node("retrieve", retrieve_node) graph$add_node("agent", agent_node) graph$add_edge("retrieve", "agent") graph$add_edge("agent", END) graph$set_entry_point("retrieve")  rag_graph <- graph$compile()  # Run it result <- rag_graph$invoke(list(   messages = list("What packages are in the tidyverse?") ))

title: RAG-Enabled Agents
text: knowledge base large, retrieved chunks may exceed LLM’s context window. context_builder() controls token budget priorities. detailed treatment priority strategies overflow behavior, see vignette("context-building"). practical example using retrieval scores priorities: builder packs items priority order budget exhausted. Dropped items reported $excluded, can log cut. Use cb_reset() turns reuse builder:
code:
cb <- context_builder(max_tokens = 500)  # System prompt gets highest priority -- always included cb <- cb_add(cb, "You are an R expert.", priority = 10, label = "system")  # Retrieved chunks get decreasing priority by relevance score hits <- retrieve(ret, "statistical models", k = 5) for (i in seq_len(nrow(hits))) {   chunk_text <- hits$id[i]  # or look up the original text   cb <- cb_add(cb, chunk_text, priority = hits$score[i], label = hits$id[i]) }  result <- cb_build(cb) cat("Included:", paste(result$included, collapse = ", "), "
") cat("Excluded:", paste(result$excluded, collapse = ", "), "
") cat("Total tokens:", result$total_tokens, "
") cb <- cb_reset(cb) # Now add fresh content for the next turn

title: RAG-Enabled Agents
text: agents need memory across sessions, knowledge_store persists JSONL file. Combined orchestr memory adapter, gives agents durable recall: agent remembers user preferences, past queries, learned facts across restarts.
code: # Knowledge store persists to disk ks <- knowledge_store$new(path = "long-term-memory.jsonl")  # Store structured facts ks$set("user.preference", list(language = "R", theme = "dark")) ks$set("session.2025-01-15", list(   topic = "regression models",   outcome = "built linear model for mtcars" ))  # Search by key pattern ks$search("^session") #> $`session.2025-01-15` #> $`session.2025-01-15`$topic #> [1] "regression models"  # Use in an orchestr agent via the adapter mem <- as_orchestr_memory(ks)

title: RAG-Enabled Agents
text: complete RAG agent combines retrieval, token management, persistent memory orchestr graph. Documents ingested local vector store, queries trigger retrieval context assembly, LLM answers using grounded context, agent persists learns future sessions. retrieval node finds chunks tidyr’s pivot_longer, context builder fits within token budget, agent answers using grounded context. query also persisted knowledge store future reference.
code:
library(securecontext) library(orchestr) library(ellmer)  # --- Knowledge base --- docs <- list(   document("dplyr provides a grammar of data manipulation with verbs like filter, select, mutate, summarise, and arrange."),   document("ggplot2 implements the grammar of graphics. Build plots layer by layer with aes(), geom_point(), geom_line(), and facet_wrap()."),   document("tidyr helps tidy data with pivot_longer, pivot_wider, separate, and unite.") )  corpus <- vapply(docs, function(d) d@text, character(1)) embedder <- embed_tfidf(corpus) vs <- vector_store$new(dims = embedder@dims) ret <- retriever(vs, embedder) add_documents(ret, docs, chunk_strategy = "sentence")  # --- Persistent memory --- ks <- knowledge_store$new(path = tempfile(fileext = ".jsonl")) mem <- as_orchestr_memory(ks)  # --- Graph nodes --- retrieve_node <- function(state, config) {   query <- state$messages[[length(state$messages)]]   result <- context_for_chat(ret, query, max_tokens = 1500, k = 3)   list(context = result$context) }  agent_node <- function(state, config) {   context <- state$context %||% ""   query <- state$messages[[length(state$messages)]]    prompt <- paste0("Context:
", context, "

Question: ", query)   chat <- chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = "Answer questions about R packages using the provided context."   )   response <- chat$chat(prompt)    # Persist what we learned   mem$set(paste0("query.", Sys.time()), query)    list(messages = list(response)) }  # --- Build and run --- schema <- state_schema(messages = "append:list", context = "character") g <- graph_builder(state_schema = schema) g$add_node("retrieve", retrieve_node) g$add_node("agent", agent_node) g$add_edge("retrieve", "agent") g$add_edge("agent", END) g$set_entry_point("retrieve")  rag <- g$compile() result <- rag$invoke(list(   messages = list("How do I reshape data from wide to long format?") ))

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) lets LLM answer questions documents (internal reports, package documentation, domain knowledge) fetching relevant passages injecting prompt. RAG tooling requires sending documents external embedding API, introduces latency, cost, privacy concerns. securecontext takes local-first approach. TF-IDF embedder runs entirely R process: API keys, network calls, data leaving machine. makes suitable air-gapped environments, privacy-sensitive workloads, rapid prototyping want results without configuring external services. sections cover complete retrieval pipeline step step. Every operation (document creation, chunking, embedding, vector search, knowledge storage, context assembly) runs locally.

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: following diagram shows data flows raw documents LLM-ready context string: left column (ingest) happens load corpus. middle column (search) runs every query. right column (assemble) packs results token budget sending LLM.
code: Ingest                      Search                     Assemble   ------                      ------                     --------   document()                  retrieve()                 context_builder()       |                           |                           |       v                           v                           v   chunk_text()              embed query               cb_add() per chunk       |                      with TF-IDF                      |       v                           |                           v   embed_tfidf()              cosine similarity          cb_build()       |                      against store                    |       v                           |                           v   vector_store$add()         ranked results             token-limited                                                         context string library(securecontext)

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: document() wraps raw text metadata auto-generated identifier. Metadata travels document pipeline, making easy trace source retrieved chunk came . Documents S7 objects, properties accessed @.
code: doc_r <- document(   "R is a programming language for statistical computing and graphics. It is widely used among statisticians and data scientists. R provides a wide variety of statistical and graphical techniques, including linear and nonlinear modelling, classical statistical tests, time-series analysis, classification, and clustering.",   metadata = list(source = "intro", topic = "R") )  doc_python <- document(   "Python is a high-level, general-purpose programming language. Its design philosophy emphasizes code readability. Python supports multiple programming paradigms, including structured, object-oriented, and functional programming. It is often used for machine learning, web development, and data analysis.",   metadata = list(source = "intro", topic = "Python") )  doc_julia <- document(   "Julia is a high-level, high-performance programming language for technical computing. It was designed for numerical analysis and computational science. Julia features optional typing, multiple dispatch, and good performance approaching that of statically-typed languages like C and Fortran.",   metadata = list(source = "intro", topic = "Julia") )  # S7 property access with @ doc_r@id #> [1] "doc_20260301203457_c6be45ee" doc_r@metadata #> $source #> [1] "intro" #>  #> $topic #> [1] "R"

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: Chunking splits long text smaller pieces suitable embedding retrieval. Smaller, focused chunks improve search precision embedder can match query specific passage rather entire document. tradeoff small chunks may lose surrounding context. securecontext offers four strategies:

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: right strategy depends content users query : Sentence chunking works best narrative text (documentation, articles) individual statements carry meaning. Queries like “dplyr ?” match well single-sentence chunks. Paragraph chunking preserves context per chunk, helps meaning spans multiple sentences. works well structured documents clear paragraph breaks. Fixed-size chunking guarantees uniform chunk lengths, useful downstream components expect consistent input sizes. overlap parameter ensures information near chunk boundaries lost. Recursive chunking robust general-purpose strategy. tries larger separators first (double newlines, single newlines, spaces, characters), producing natural-looking chunks respect document structure. good default unsure. Fixed-size chunking useful need consistent chunk lengths, example working models expect uniform input sizes. overlap parameter creates sliding window, ensuring information near chunk boundaries appears adjacent chunks:
code:
# Sentence-level chunking sentences <- chunk_text(doc_r@text, strategy = "sentence") cat("Sentence chunks:", length(sentences), "
") #> Sentence chunks: 3 sentences #> [1] "R is a programming language for statistical computing and graphics."                                                                                                                                #> [2] "It is widely used among statisticians and data scientists."                                                                                                                                         #> [3] "R provides
a wide variety of statistical and graphical techniques, including
linear and nonlinear modelling, classical statistical tests, time-series
analysis, classification, and clustering."  # Recursive chunking with a small max_size to demonstrate splitting small_chunks <- chunk_text(doc_r@text, strategy = "recursive", max_size = 120) cat("
Recursive chunks (max 120 chars):", length(small_chunks), "
") #>  #> Recursive chunks (max 120 chars): 5 small_chunks #> [1] "R is a programming language for statistical computing and graphics."      #> [2] "It is widely used among statisticians and data scientists. R provides"    #> [3] "a wide variety of statistical and graphical techniques, including"        #> [4] "linear and nonlinear modelling, classical statistical tests, time-series" #> [5] "analysis, classification, and clustering." long_text <- paste(   "The tidyverse is a collection of R packages designed for data science.",   "It includes ggplot2 for visualization, dplyr for data manipulation,",   "tidyr for data tidying, readr for reading data, purrr for functional",   "programming, tibble for modern data frames, stringr for string",   "manipulation, and forcats for factor handling.",   "All tidyverse packages share an underlying design philosophy,",   "grammar, and data structures." )  fixed_chunks <- chunk_fixed(long_text, size = 100, overlap = 20) cat("Fixed chunks (size=100, overlap=20):", length(fixed_chunks), "

") #> Fixed chunks (size=100, overlap=20): 5 for (i in seq_along(fixed_chunks)) {   cat(sprintf("Chunk %d (%d chars): %s

", i, nchar(fixed_chunks[i]), fixed_chunks[i])) } #> Chunk 1 (100 chars): The tidyverse is a collection of R packages designed for data science. It includes ggplot2 for visua #>  #> Chunk 2 (100 chars): es ggplot2 for visualization, dplyr for data manipulation, tidyr for data tidying, readr for reading #>  #> Chunk 3 (100 chars): g, readr for reading data, purrr for functional programming, tibble for modern data frames, stringr  #>  #> Chunk 4 (100 chars): ata frames, stringr for string manipulation, and forcats for factor handling. All tidyverse packages #>  #> Chunk 5 (89 chars): l tidyverse packages share an underlying design philosophy, grammar, and data structures.

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: Embeddings numerical representations text capture semantic similarity. Texts similar topics produce vectors close together embedding space, enabling search meaning rather exact keywords. embed_tfidf() builds vocabulary corpus returns embedder can project new texts TF-IDF space. TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) weighs words important specific document relative corpus. Common words like “” get low weights; distinctive words like “regression” get high weights. Everything runs locally; API keys required.
code: # Gather all document texts as the training corpus corpus <- c(doc_r@text, doc_python@text, doc_julia@text)  embedder <- embed_tfidf(corpus)  # The embedder is an S7 object; dims equals the vocabulary size cat("Embedding dimensions:", embedder@dims, "\n") #> Embedding dimensions: 79  # Embed a new query query_matrix <- embed_texts(embedder, "statistical analysis in R") cat("Query embedding shape:", nrow(query_matrix), "x", ncol(query_matrix), "\n") #> Query embedding shape: 1 x 79

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: vector_store R6 class providing -memory cosine-similarity search optional RDS persistence. stores embedding vectors keyed ID retrieves closest matches query vector. Since R6, use $ method access. Cosine similarity measures angle two vectors, ignoring magnitude. score 1.0 means identical direction (maximum similarity); 0.0 means orthogonal (similarity). Persistence straightforward $save() $load(). lets build embedding index reuse across R sessions without re-embedding corpus:
code: vs <- vector_store$new(dims = embedder@dims)  # Embed and store each document manually ids <- c("r", "python", "julia") embeddings <- embed_texts(embedder, corpus) vs$add(ids, embeddings)  cat("Store size:", vs$size(), "vectors\n") #> Store size: 3 vectors  # Search for the closest match to a query query_emb <- embed_texts(embedder, "data science and statistics") results <- vs$search(query_emb, k = 3) print(results) #>       id      score #> 1 python 0.07428944 #> 2      r 0.06359400 #> 3  julia 0.00000000 tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds") vs$save(tmp)  vs2 <- vector_store$new(dims = embedder@dims) vs2$load(tmp) cat("Loaded store size:", vs2$size(), "\n") #> Loaded store size: 3  # Clean up unlink(tmp)

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: previous steps (chunk, embed, store, search) building blocks. retriever() combines vector store embedder single object handles full ingest--search workflow. Use add_documents() chunk, embed, store documents one call, retrieve() search. recommended interface applications. reduces boilerplate ensures chunking embedding stay consistent ingest query time. returned data frame contains chunk IDs cosine similarity scores. Higher scores indicate greater relevance. can use scores directly priorities context builder (see vignette("context-building")).
code: # Fresh store for the retriever vs_ret <- vector_store$new(dims = embedder@dims) ret <- retriever(vs_ret, embedder)  # add_documents handles chunking + embedding internally docs <- list(doc_r, doc_python, doc_julia) add_documents(ret, docs, chunk_strategy = "sentence")  cat("Chunks in store:", vs_ret$size(), "\n\n") #> Chunks in store: 10  # Retrieve the top 3 chunks for a query hits <- retrieve(ret, "machine learning", k = 3) print(hits) #>                                    id     score #> 1 doc_20260301203457_e35844e7_chunk_4 0.3992843 #> 2 doc_20260301203457_c6be45ee_chunk_1 0.0000000 #> 3 doc_20260301203457_c6be45ee_chunk_2 0.0000000

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: knowledge_store R6 class providing persistent key-value storage backed JSONL. vector store optimized similarity search embeddings, knowledge store designed structured data look key: agent memory, user preferences, session history, learned facts. JSONL format (one JSON object per line) makes store append-friendly human-readable. Data persists across R sessions automatically.
code: ks <- knowledge_store$new(path = tempfile(fileext = ".jsonl"))  # Store some facts ks$set("lang.r", list(type = "statistical", year = 1993)) ks$set("lang.python", list(type = "general-purpose", year = 1991)) ks$set("lang.julia", list(type = "numerical", year = 2012)) ks$set("user.preference", "R")  cat("Total entries:", ks$size(), "\n") #> Total entries: 4  # Retrieve a value ks$get("lang.r") #> $type #> [1] "statistical" #>  #> $year #> [1] 1993  # Search keys by regex ks$search("^lang") #> [1] "lang.r"      "lang.python" "lang.julia"  # List all keys ks$list() #> [1] "lang.r"          "lang.python"     "lang.julia"      "user.preference"  # Clean up unlink(ks$.__enclos_env__$private$.path)

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: context_builder() assembles token-limited context string multiple sources, prioritizing important content. final step sending context LLM: keeps within model’s context window including relevant information. priority strategies, overflow behavior, multi-turn patterns, see vignette("context-building"). builder processes items priority order stops adding token budget exhausted. Items fit reported $excluded, making easy log dropped. Use cb_reset() clear items reuse builder new turn conversation:
code:
cb <- context_builder(max_tokens = 100)  # Add content with different priorities (higher = included first) cb <- cb_add(cb, "You are a helpful assistant.", priority = 10, label = "system") cb <- cb_add(cb,   "R is great for statistics and data visualization.",   priority = 5, label = "retrieved_chunk_1" ) cb <- cb_add(cb,   "Python is popular for machine learning and web development.",   priority = 4, label = "retrieved_chunk_2" ) cb <- cb_add(cb,   "Julia offers high performance for numerical computing workloads.",   priority = 3, label = "retrieved_chunk_3" )  result <- cb_build(cb)  cat("Assembled context:
") #> Assembled context: cat(result$context, "

") #> You are a helpful assistant. #>  #> R is great for statistics and data visualization. #>  #> Python is popular for machine learning and web development. #>  #> Julia offers high performance for numerical computing workloads. cat("Included:", paste(result$included, collapse = ", "), "
") #> Included: system, retrieved_chunk_1, retrieved_chunk_2, retrieved_chunk_3 cat("Excluded:", paste(result$excluded, collapse = ", "), "
") #> Excluded: cat("Total tokens:", result$total_tokens, "
") #> Total tokens: 41 cb2 <- cb_reset(cb) cb2 <- cb_add(cb2, "New system prompt.", priority = 10, label = "system_v2") result2 <- cb_build(cb2) cat("After reset -- included:", paste(result2$included, collapse = ", "), "
") #> After reset -- included: system_v2

what: Full pipeline: retrieve and build context
title: Retrieval Workflows
text: context_for_chat() combines retrieval context building single call. Given retriever query, retrieves top-k chunks packs token-limited context string. typical integration point agent: retrieve relevant information, assemble context window, pass LLM provider.
code:
context_result <- context_for_chat(ret, "statistical computing", max_tokens = 2000, k = 5)  cat("Context for LLM:
") #> Context for LLM: cat(context_result$context, "

") #> R is a programming language for statistical computing and graphics. #>  #> R provides #> a wide variety of statistical and graphical techniques, including #> linear and nonlinear modelling, classical statistical tests, time-series #> analysis, classification, and clustering. #>  #> It is widely used among statisticians and data scientists. #>  #> Python is a high-level, general-purpose programming language. #>  #> Its design philosophy emphasizes code readability. cat("Included chunks:", length(context_result$included), "
") #> Included chunks: 5 cat("Total tokens:", context_result$total_tokens, "
") #> Total tokens: 73

title: Retrieval Workflows
text: securecontext retrieval pipeline follows steps: document() – wrap text metadata chunk_text() – split retrieval units embed_tfidf() – build local embedder corpus vector_store$new() – store search embeddings retriever() + add_documents() – high-level ingest retrieve() – semantic search knowledge_store$new() – persistent key-value memory context_builder() + cb_add() + cb_build() – token-aware assembly context_for_chat() – one-call retrieve--build context building strategies, see vignette("context-building"). wire retrieval orchestr agent graphs, see vignette("orchestr-integration").

title: Getting Started with securecontext
text: Large language models know trained . need LLM answer questions documents (internal reports, package documentation, domain-specific knowledge), feed information model’s context window. pattern called Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). securecontext implements complete, local-first RAG pipeline R. Every component (chunking, embedding, vector search, context assembly) runs entirely machine external API calls. documents never leave environment. package also token-aware. LLMs finite context windows, naively stuffing retrieved text prompt wastes tokens overflows limit. securecontext’s context builder packs important information first priority-based budget reports included dropped.

what: The RAG pipeline at a glance
title: Getting Started with securecontext
text: following diagram shows end--end flow raw documents LLM-ready context string: step independently useful, compose naturally pipeline. sections cover three building blocks: documents, chunking, knowledge store. full retrieval pipeline, see vignette("retrieval-workflows").
code: Documents     Chunk        Embed       Store        Query       Context     LLM  ---------    -------      -------     -------      -------     ---------   -----  |  doc  | -> | sent | ->  | TF- | ->  | vec | <-   | "my  | -> | token | -> | R |  | text  |    | para |     | IDF |     | tor |      | query|    | aware |    |   |  |       |    | rec  |     |     |     | str |      |      |    | build |    |   |  ---------    -------      -------     -------      -------     ---------   -----  document()  chunk_text() embed_tfidf  vector_store retrieve()  context_    chat()                            ()          $new()                   builder()

title: Getting Started with securecontext
text: document() function wraps raw text metadata auto-generated identifier. Documents unit ingestion throughout package. Chunking splits long text smaller pieces suitable embedding retrieval. Smaller chunks improve search precision embedder can match query focused passages rather entire documents. securecontext ships four chunking strategies, suited different content types: choice strategy depends content. Sentence chunking works well narrative text individual statements carry meaning. Recursive chunking robust mixed content tries larger separators first (double newlines, single newlines, spaces) falling back character splits.
code: library(securecontext)  doc <- document(   "R is a language for statistical computing. It has many packages for data analysis. The tidyverse is a popular collection of packages.",   metadata = list(source = "intro") )  # Different chunking strategies chunks_sent <- chunk_text(doc$text, strategy = "sentence") chunks_para <- chunk_text(doc$text, strategy = "paragraph") chunks_rec  <- chunk_text(doc$text, strategy = "recursive", max_size = 50)

title: Getting Started with securecontext
text: knowledge_store persistent key-value store backed JSONL file. Unlike vector store (optimized similarity search), knowledge store holds structured facts look key: user preferences, session state, agent memory. Stored data survives across R sessions, making suitable agents need durable memory. Keys plain strings, values can R object jsonlite can serialize (lists, vectors, data frames). $search() method accepts regular expression pattern find keys prefix pattern.
code: ks <- knowledge_store$new(path = tempfile(fileext = ".jsonl"))  ks$set("user.name", "Alice") ks$set("user.preferences", list(theme = "dark", lang = "R"))  ks$get("user.name") ks$search("^user")

title: Getting Started with securecontext
text: documents, chunking strategies, knowledge store place, rest securecontext documentation covers full pipeline: vignette("retrieval-workflows"): Build complete RAG pipeline TF-IDF embeddings, vector search, retrievers, context_for_chat() convenience function. vignette("context-building"): token-aware context builder depth, covering priority budgets, overflow behavior, multi-source assembly. vignette("orchestr-integration"): Wire retrieval orchestr agent graphs memory adapter retrieve--generate patterns.

what: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
text: [!CAUTION] Alpha software. package part broader effort Ian Flores Siaca develop proper AI infrastructure R ecosystem. active development used production official release published. APIs may change without notice. Memory, knowledge persistence, RAG retrieval, context management R LLM agents.

title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
text: RAG solutions LLM agents require sending documents external embedding APIs. securecontext takes different approach: builds local TF-IDF embeddings entirely R, external API calls data leaving machine. package provides token-aware chunking respects LLM context windows, splitting documents sentence, paragraph, recursively chunks fit within token budgets. built-knowledge store JSONL persistence lets agents retrieve relevant context across sessions without relying third-party services. Everything runs locally, making suitable sensitive data, air-gapped environments, workflows data privacy matters.

title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
text: securecontext part 7-package ecosystem building governed AI agents R: securecontext provides memory retrieval layer agents. sits alongside securetools secureguard middle tier, giving agents ability chunk documents, build TF-IDF embeddings locally, retrieve relevant context LLM prompts.

title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ian-flores/securecontext")

title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
text: Document chunking – fixed-size, sentence, paragraph, recursive strategies TF-IDF embeddings – local embeddings external API required Vector store – -memory cosine similarity search RDS persistence Knowledge store – persistent JSONL key-value storage Semantic retrieval – query documents meaning Context builder – token-aware priority-based context assembly Integration helpers – works orchestr ellmer

title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
text: Split text manageable pieces using one four strategies. chunk_text() dispatches appropriate strategy function:
code:
library(securecontext)  text <- "First paragraph with several sentences.

Second paragraph here.

Third."  # Sentence-level splitting chunk_text(text, strategy = "sentence") #> [1] "First paragraph with several sentences." "Second paragraph here." #> [3] "Third."  # Paragraph-level splitting chunk_text(text, strategy = "paragraph") #> [1] "First paragraph with several sentences." "Second paragraph here." #> [3] "Third."  # Fixed-size chunks with overlap chunk_fixed(paste(rep("word", 200), collapse = " "), size = 100, overlap = 10)  # Recursive splitting (tries paragraph -> newline -> sentence -> space) chunk_recursive(text, max_size = 80)

title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
text: persistent JSONL key-value store agent memory. Entries keyed strings optional metadata timestamps:
code: # In-memory store ks <- knowledge_store$new()  # Persistent store backed by a JSONL file ks <- knowledge_store$new(path = "agent-memory.jsonl")  # Store and retrieve values ks$set("user_preference", "dark mode", metadata = list(source = "onboarding")) ks$get("user_preference") #> [1] "dark mode"  # Search keys by regex ks$search("user_") #> [1] "user_preference"  # List all keys and check size ks$list() ks$size()

title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
text: Assemble token-aware context LLM prompts. Higher-priority items included first; lower-priority items dropped token budget exceeded:
code: cb <- context_builder(max_tokens = 200) cb <- cb_add(cb, "System instructions go here.", priority = 10, label = "system") cb <- cb_add(cb, "Relevant retrieved passage.", priority = 5, label = "rag") cb <- cb_add(cb, "Nice-to-have background info.", priority = 1, label = "background")  result <- cb_build(cb) result$context       # assembled text, highest priority first result$included      # labels of items that fit result$excluded      # labels of items that were dropped result$total_tokens  # token count of the assembled context

title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
code: library(securecontext)  # Create documents docs <- list(   document("R is great for statistics.", metadata = list(topic = "R")),   document("Python excels at machine learning.", metadata = list(topic = "Python")) )  # Build embeddings and index documents emb <- embed_tfidf(vapply(docs, `[[`, character(1), "text")) vs <- vector_store$new(dims = emb$dims) ret <- retriever(vs, emb) add_documents(ret, docs)  # Retrieve relevant context result <- context_for_chat(ret, "statistical computing", max_tokens = 2000) cat(result$context)

title: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents
text: securecontext ships three vignettes covering common workflows: Getting Started securecontext – package overview, core API, basic retrieval (vignette("securecontext")) Retrieval Workflows – end--end RAG patterns, chunking strategies, vector store persistence (vignette("retrieval-workflows")) RAG-Enabled Agents – integrating securecontext orchestr agent memory context injection (vignette("orchestr-integration")) Full reference documentation available pkgdown site.

code: emb <- embed_tfidf(c("cat sat on mat", "dog ran in park")) vs <- vector_store$new(dims = emb@dims) ret <- retriever(vs, emb) add_documents(ret, document("The cat sat on the mat.")) result <- context_for_chat(ret, "cat", max_tokens = 100, k = 2) result$context #> [1] "The cat sat on the mat."

code: # Create a simple random embedder random_embed <- function(texts) matrix(runif(length(texts) * 3), ncol = 3) emb <- new_embedder(random_embed, dims = 3L) emb@dims #> [1] 3

title: Retrieve relevant chunks — retrieve
code: emb <- embed_tfidf(c("cat sat on mat", "dog ran in park")) vs <- vector_store$new(dims = emb@dims) ret <- retriever(vs, emb) add_documents(ret, document("The cat sat on the mat.")) retrieve(ret, "cat", k = 1) #>                                    id     score #> 1 doc_20260301203451_92169253_chunk_1 0.5773503

what: securecontext: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents — securecontext-package
title: securecontext: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents — securecontext-package
text: Provides memory, knowledge persistence, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) retrieval, token-aware context window management Large Language Model (LLM) agents. Includes document chunking, Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) embeddings, vector search, persistent key-value knowledge stores, priority-based context assembly.

title: securecontext: Memory, Knowledge Persistence, and Context Management for Large Language Model Agents — securecontext-package
text: Maintainer: Ian Flores Siaca iflores.siaca@hey.com [copyright holder]

title: securecontext 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN release. S7-based securecontext_document class representing text documents metadata. chunk_text() splitting documents overlapping chunks token count. TF-IDF embedder (embed_tfidf()) local, dependency-free text embeddings. vector_store R6 class cosine similarity search RDS persistence. knowledge_store R6 class persistent JSONL key-value storage. context_builder token-aware, priority-based context assembly. count_tokens() utility using word-based approximation. Optional integration ‘ellmer’ ‘orchestr’ LLM agent workflows.
